text: Six types of load progression criteria in lower limb tendinopathy exercise programmes fall into two categories: pain as a primary criterion (evoking-pain and avoid-pain based) and pain/symptom control as a secondary criterion (conditioning stages, fatigue-based, subjective perception, and temporary linear increase).
population: patients with Achilles, patellar, or gluteal tendinopathy

text: Pain-based criteria are the most commonly used progression method in lower limb tendinopathy loading programmes, applied in the majority of the 30 RCTs reviewed.
population: patients with Achilles, patellar, or gluteal tendinopathy

text: The predominant use of pain-based progression criteria in tendinopathy exercise programmes is not supported by strong evidence, despite being widely adopted in clinical practice.
population: patients with Achilles, patellar, or gluteal tendinopathy

text: Conditioning-stage-based progression (structured phases of increasing difficulty) is the second most commonly applied criterion for load progression in tendinopathy rehabilitation.
population: patients with Achilles, patellar, or gluteal tendinopathy

text: Fatigue-based criteria, temporary linear load increases, and subjective perception of patient abilities are only occasionally used as progression criteria in tendinopathy loading programmes, despite being viable alternatives to pain-based approaches.
population: patients with Achilles, patellar, or gluteal tendinopathy

text: No comparative studies exist that test the same tendinopathy exercise programme using different progression criteria, meaning the optimal method for progressing load remains unknown.
population: patients with Achilles, patellar, or gluteal tendinopathy
